questions.text:
Rishav can make a round-trip to Jadavpur from College Street to do a task that takes 1 hour to complete and gives him utility worth Rs 500. Rishav ONLY has the following two ways to commute to Jadavpur from College Street:
1. Rishav can purchase a return ticket for the metro, which costs Rs 50 and results in a total commute time of 1 hour. Thus, if Rishav takes metro to travel to Jadavpur, it takes him 2 hours to complete the task.
2. Another option is to travel using a city bus, which costs Rs 30 in total, resulting in a total commute time of 3 hours. Thus, if Rishav takes city bus to travel to Jadavpur, it takes him 4 hours to complete the task.
When Rishav is not traveling or working on the specific task, he can work and earn Rs 100 per hour.

What is the opportunity cost of traveling by bus?
options: Rs 200
Rs 650
Rs 150
Rs 230
questions.explanation: The opportunity cost of the bus option includes its direct cost (Rs 30) plus the value of the additional time spent compared to the metro (2 extra hours at Rs 100/hr).

questions.text: What is the likely immediate effect on the demand for flats in the current market when buyers expect prices to rise significantly next year?
options: Demand will decrease as buyers wait for prices to increase next year.
Demand will increase as buyers rush to buy now before prices increase.
Demand will remain the same as buyers are indifferent to future price changes.
Demand will fluctuate based on the overall economic outlook.
questions.explanation: Expectations of higher future prices cause consumers to increase current demand to avoid paying more later.

questions.text: You are constructing an apartment building, and your market research department initially estimated revenues of Rs 9 Crore, while the engineering department estimated costs of Rs 6 crore, of which spending Rs. 1.5 comes from laying the foundation. After spending Rs. 1.5 crore on the foundation, a recession causes the revenue estimate to be revised down to Rs. 5.0 crore. Given this situation, should you complete the apartment building?
options: Yes, because the project will still generate positive revenue.
No, because the revised revenue estimate is lower than the total cost of construction.
Yes, because you have already spent Rs. 1.5 crore, so completing the project is the best option.
No, because the recession has caused a loss, and it’s better to cut losses now.
questions.explanation: Ignore the Rs 1.5 crore sunk cost. The marginal revenue (5.0) exceeds the remaining marginal cost (4.5).

questions.text: What would be your recommendation for the firm in Q6?

questions.text:
Let there be three types of consumers: High, Medium, and Low. Banana, a software company has a product called Pro-Max, which is produced at zero marginal cost. There are 50 High type (WTP: 120), 30 Medium type (WTP: 60), and 20 Low type (WTP: 20) consumers. 

The uniform price (or a single price) that gives Banana, the highest revenue is
options: None of the above
questions.explanation: At price 120, revenue is 120*50=6000. At price 60, revenue is 60*(50+30)=4800. At price 20, revenue is 20*(50+30+20)=2000. Thus, 120 maximizes revenue.

questions.text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. If the industry is perfectly competitive, what will be the equilibrium quantity and price?
questions.explanation: In competition, P = MC, so 120 - 2Q = 4Q, which gives Q = 20 and P = 80.

original_data.question_text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. If the industry is perfectly competitive, what will be the equilibrium quantity and price?
original_data.explanation: In competition, P = MC, so 120 - 2Q = 4Q, which gives Q = 20 and P = 80.

questions.text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. What is the total profit in perfect competition (at Q=20, P=80)?

original_data.question_text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. What is the total profit in perfect competition (at Q=20, P=80)?

questions.text: A competitive firm’s short-run supply curve is its _________ cost curve above its _________ cost curve.
options: average-total; marginal
average-variable; marginal
marginal; average-total
marginal; average-variable
questions.explanation: In the short run, a perfectly competitive firm will produce where price equals marginal cost, provided the price is above the minimum average variable cost.

original_data.question_text: A competitive firm’s short-run supply curve is its _________ cost curve above its _________ cost curve.

options.A: average-total; marginal
options.B: average-variable; marginal
options.C: marginal; average-total
options.D: marginal; average-variable

original_data.explanation: In the short run, a perfectly competitive firm will produce where price equals marginal cost, provided the price is above the minimum average variable cost.

questions.text: Growers expect that the price of a bushel of wheat will increase in the coming month. This belief results in
options: an increase in current supply of wheat
a decrease in current supply of wheat
a decrease in future supply of wheat
no change in current or future supply of wheat
questions.explanation: Expected future price increases lead sellers to withhold current supply to take advantage of higher prices later.

original_data.question_text: Growers expect that the price of a bushel of wheat will increase in the coming month. This belief results in

options.A: an increase in current supply of wheat
options.B: a decrease in current supply of wheat
options.C: a decrease in future supply of wheat
options.D: no change in current or future supply of wheat

original_data.explanation: Expected future price increases lead sellers to withhold current supply to take advantage of higher prices later.

questions.text: If the price of both a substitute and a complement for commodity X increases, the demand for X will:
options: Increase
Decrease
Remain unchanged
Any of the above, depending on the situation
questions.explanation: An increase in a substitute's price raises demand for X, while an increase in a complement's price lowers it; the final direction depends on which effect is stronger.

original_data.question_text: If the price of both a substitute and a complement for commodity X increases, the demand for X will:

options.A: Increase
options.B: Decrease
options.C: Remain unchanged
options.D: Any of the above, depending on the situation

original_data.explanation: An increase in a substitute's price raises demand for X, while an increase in a complement's price lowers it; the final direction depends on which effect is stronger.

raw_questions_dump.question_text:
Rishav can make a round-trip to Jadavpur from College Street to do a task that takes 1 hour to complete and gives him utility worth Rs 500. Rishav ONLY has the following two ways to commute to Jadavpur from College Street:
1. Rishav can purchase a return ticket for the metro, which costs Rs 50 and results in a total commute time of 1 hour. Thus, if Rishav takes metro to travel to Jadavpur, it takes him 2 hours to complete the task.
2. Another option is to travel using a city bus, which costs Rs 30 in total, resulting in a total commute time of 3 hours. Thus, if Rishav takes city bus to travel to Jadavpur, it takes him 4 hours to complete the task.
When Rishav is not traveling or working on the specific task, he can work and earn Rs 100 per hour.

What is the opportunity cost of traveling by bus?

options.A: Rs 200
options.B: Rs 650
options.C: Rs 150
options.D: Rs 230

raw_questions_dump.explanation: The opportunity cost of the bus option includes its direct cost (Rs 30) plus the value of the additional time spent compared to the metro (2 extra hours at Rs 100/hr).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the Opportunity cost of travelling by metro?

options.A: Rs 200
options.B: Rs 250
options.C: Rs 470
options.D: Rs 230

raw_questions_dump.explanation: The opportunity cost includes the explicit ticket cost (Rs 50) and the implicit cost of time for the commute and task (2 hours at Rs 100/hr).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the net economic benefit of Rishav by travelling using metro?

options.A: Rs 650
options.B: Rs 180
options.C: Rs 250
options.D: Rs 130

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Net economic benefit is the total utility (Rs 500) minus the total economic cost (Rs 250).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the new opportunity cost of traveling by bus for Debayan, who is identical to Rishav except that he can earn hourly wage of only Rs 50 when not traveling?

options.A: Rs 550
options.B: Rs 250
options.C: Rs 470
options.D: Rs 200

raw_questions_dump.explanation: At a wage of Rs 50, the time cost for the 4-hour bus trip is Rs 200.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the likely immediate effect on the demand for flats in the current market when buyers expect prices to rise significantly next year?

options.A: Demand will decrease as buyers wait for prices to increase next year.
options.B: Demand will increase as buyers rush to buy now before prices increase.
options.C: Demand will remain the same as buyers are indifferent to future price changes.
options.D: Demand will fluctuate based on the overall economic outlook.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Expectations of higher future prices cause consumers to increase current demand to avoid paying more later.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: How will the supply of flats in the current market likely change when sellers expect prices to rise significantly next year?

options.A: Supply will increase as sellers try to sell flats quickly before prices fall.
options.B: Supply will remain the same, as sellers are unsure about future price movements.
options.C: Supply will decrease as sellers decide to hold onto their flats in anticipation of higher prices next year.
options.D: Supply will decrease due to a reduction in the number of flats being built.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Sellers will hoard inventory today to sell at the expected higher prices in the future, decreasing current supply.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the likely impact on the price of flats in the current market if both demand increases and supply decreases due to expectations of higher future prices?

options.A: Prices will decrease as the market is flooded with buyers.
options.B: Prices will increase due to higher demand and lower supply.
options.C: Prices will remain stable as the effects of increased demand and decreased supply cancel each other out.
options.D: Prices will fluctuate unpredictably depending on the number of flats available.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Simultaneous increase in demand and decrease in supply creates upward pressure on the equilibrium price.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If sellers hold off on selling their flats due to expectations of higher prices next year, what is the likely short-term impact on the market?

options.A: The number of flats available for sale will increase in the short term.
options.B: There will be a shortage of flats in the market, driving up prices.
options.C: Prices will decrease due to an oversupply of flats.
options.D: Prices will remain unchanged, as the market adjusts to the lower supply.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Reduced current supply coupled with potentially higher demand leads to a market shortage and rising prices.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What occurs when the market price of a product falls below its equilibrium price?

options.A: The quantity demanded matches the quantity supplied, ensuring market equilibrium.
options.B: The quantity demanded exceeds the quantity supplied.
options.C: The quantity demanded is less than the quantity supplied.
options.D: This situation is impossible because prices always adjust to the equilibrium level.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: When the price is below equilibrium, consumers want more than producers provide, creating excess demand.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the cross-price elasticity of demand between two goods is POSITIVE, what does this indicate about their relationship?

options.A: The goods are substitutes
options.B: The goods are complements
options.C: The goods are unrelated
options.D: The goods are inferior

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A positive cross-price elasticity means that as the price of one good rises, the demand for the other increases, characterizing substitutes.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What does a perfectly vertical demand curve for a commodity indicate?

options.A: Demand is highly elastic
options.B: Demand is perfectly inelastic
options.C: Demand is unitary elastic
options.D: Demand is perfectly elastic

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A vertical demand curve signifies that the quantity demanded remains unchanged regardless of the price level.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the effect of an increase in the supply of a commodity on equilibrium?

options.A: It leads to a higher equilibrium price and lower equilibrium quantity
options.B: It leads to a lower equilibrium price and higher equilibrium quantity
options.C: It causes no change in equilibrium price or quantity
options.D: It leads to a higher equilibrium price and higher equilibrium quantity

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A rightward shift in supply lowers the equilibrium price and increases the equilibrium quantity.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the percentage increase in the quantity of a commodity demanded is smaller than the percentage fall in price, the price elasticity of demand is:

options.A: Greater than 1
options.B: Equal to 1
options.C: Less than 1
options.D: Zero

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Elasticity is less than 1 (inelastic) when the quantity responsiveness is smaller than the price change.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the free-market wage rate and employment level?

options.A: Rs. 4 per hour, 48 million hours
options.B: Rs. 6 per hour, 60 million hours
options.C: Rs. 5 per hour, 50 million hours
options.D: Rs. 7 per hour, 72 million hours

raw_questions_dump.explanation: This represents the equilibrium point where labor supply equals labor demand in the absence of intervention.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the government sets a minimum wage of $6 per hour, how many people will be employed?

options.A: 40 million hours
options.B: 60 million hours
options.C: 24 million hours
options.D: 50 million hours

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A minimum wage above equilibrium creates a labor surplus, and employment is determined by the lower quantity demanded by firms.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Instead of the minimum wage, the government introduces labor subsidies, where each employer is paid a subsidy of Rs 2 for every hour of labor done by the employee. What will the new equilibrium wage and employment level be?

options.A: Rs 5 per hour, 60 million hours
options.B: Rs 6 per hour, 72 million hours
options.C: Rs 7 per hour, 84 million hours
options.D: Rs. 4 per hour, 48 million hours

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Subsidies increase the demand for labor, shifting the equilibrium to a higher wage and higher employment level.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the government wants to maximum the revenue from taxing the consumers, which type of good should it tax?

options.A: A good with perfectly elastic demand
options.B: A good with unitary elastic demand
options.C: A good with perfectly inelastic demand
options.D: A luxury good with many substitutes

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Taxing goods with perfectly inelastic demand results in no change in quantity demanded, maximizing tax revenue.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A company raises the price of its product by 15%, and total revenue increases. What does this suggest about the price elasticity of demand?

options.A: Demand is elastic
options.B: Demand is inelastic
options.C: Demand is unitary elastic
options.D: Demand is perfectly elastic

raw_questions_dump.explanation: If revenue and price move in the same direction, the demand is inelastic.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Find Average Total Cost (ATC) for 6 units of Ice Cream.

options.A: Rs. 5
options.B: Rs. 7
options.C: Rs. 70
options.D: Rs. 12

raw_questions_dump.explanation: ATC = Total Cost / Quantity. At 6 units, TC is Rs 420, so 420 / 6 = 70.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the Marginal Cost (MC) when the production increases from 4 units to 5 units of ice cream?

options.A: Rs. 10
options.B: Rs. 80
options.C: Rs. 40
options.D: Rs. 50

raw_questions_dump.explanation: MC is the change in Total Cost. TC at 5 units (310) - TC at 4 units (230) = 80.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: At which level of output does the Average Total Cost start increasing?

options.A: 3 units
options.B: 4 units
options.C: 5 units
options.D: 6 units

raw_questions_dump.explanation: ATC decreases until it reaches its minimum (MC = ATC) and starts increasing thereafter; here it rises after Q=3.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: You are constructing an apartment building, and your market research department initially estimated revenues of Rs 9 Crore, while the engineering department estimated costs of Rs 6 crore, of which spending Rs. 1.5 comes from laying the foundation. After spending Rs. 1.5 crore on the foundation, a recession causes the revenue estimate to be revised down to Rs. 5.0 crore. Given this situation, should you complete the apartment building?

options.A: Yes, because the project will still generate positive revenue.
options.B: No, because the revised revenue estimate is lower than the total cost of construction.
options.C: Yes, because you have already spent Rs. 1.5 crore, so completing the project is the best option.
options.D: No, because the recession has caused a loss, and it’s better to cut losses now.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Ignore the Rs 1.5 crore sunk cost. The marginal revenue (5.0) exceeds the remaining marginal cost (4.5).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A competitive firm’s short-run supply curve is its _________ cost curve above its _________ cost curve.

options.A: average-total; marginal
options.B: average-variable; marginal
options.C: marginal; average-total
options.D: marginal; average-variable

raw_questions_dump.explanation: In the short run, a firm will produce where P=MC as long as the price is above the average variable cost.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A profit-maximizing firm in a competitive market is currently producing 100 units of output. It has average total cost of $8, and fixed cost of $200. Suppose the average revenue is $7. What is its average variable cost?

options.D: None of the above

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What would be your recommendation for the firm in Q6?

options.A: keep producing in the short run but exit the market in the long run.
options.B: shut down in the short run but return to production in the long run.
options.C: shut down in the short run and exit the market in the long run.
options.D: keep producing both in the short run and in the long run.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Since Price ($7) > AVC ($6), the firm should operate in the short run, but since Price < ATC ($8), it should exit in the long run.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the company’s Fixed Cost? (Given TC = 200 + 55q)
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Fixed cost is the constant term in the total cost function that does not vary with quantity q.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the company produces 100,000 units of goods (q = 100), what would be its Average Variable Cost (AVC)?
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Variable Cost (VC) = 55q. AVC = VC / q = 55.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The government imposes a $1,000 per year license fee on all pizza restaurants. As a result, which cost curves shift?

options.A: average total cost and marginal cost
options.B: average total cost and average fixed cost
options.C: average variable cost and marginal cost
options.D: average variable cost and average fixed cost

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A flat license fee increases fixed costs, which shifts the average fixed cost and average total cost curves upward.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: For a profit-maximizing monopoly that charges a single price, what is the relationship between price P, marginal revenue MR, and marginal cost MC?

options.A: P = MR and MR = MC.
options.B: P > MR and MR = MC.
options.C: P = MR and MR > MC
options.D: P > MR and MR > MC.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Monopolists set production where MR=MC, but since they face a downward-sloping demand curve, the price is higher than MR.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If a monopoly’s fixed costs increase, its price will _________ and its profit will _________.

options.A: increase; decrease
options.B: decrease; increase
options.C: increase; stay the same
options.D: stay the same; decrease

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Fixed costs do not affect Marginal Cost, which determines price, so the price remains unchanged while total profit falls.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Which of the following statements about price discrimination is true?

options.A: Price discrimination increases consumer surplus.
options.B: Price discrimination is only possible in perfectly competitive markets.
options.C: Price discrimination allows firms to capture more of the total surplus.
options.D: None of the above.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Price discrimination transfers surplus from consumers to producers, increasing the firm's share of total surplus.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A natural monopoly exists when:

options.A: The government restricts entry into the industry
options.B: The firm has control over a key input
options.C: One firm can supply the entire market at a lower cost than multiple firms
options.D: The firm earns excessive profits

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Natural monopolies occur when economies of scale allow a single firm to produce the total market output more efficiently than several firms.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Price discrimination is more likely to be successful when:

options.A: Consumers can easily resell the product
options.B: Consumers have the same willingness to pay
options.C: The firm has market power and can prevent resale
options.D: The market is perfectly competitive

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Effective price discrimination requires market power to set different prices and the ability to prevent arbitrage (resale).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the total revenue when the firm sells 3 units? (Fixed cost=5, MC=6, Price at 3 units=8)

options.A: Rs. 10
options.B: Rs. 24
options.C: Rs. 30
options.D: Rs. 40

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Total Revenue = Price * Quantity. At 3 units, Price is Rs 8, so 8 * 3 = 24.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the marginal revenue when output increases from 2 to 3 units? (Price at 2 units=9, Price at 3 units=8)

options.A: Rs. 6
options.B: Rs. 7
options.C: Rs. 8
options.D: Rs. 9

raw_questions_dump.explanation: TR(2) = 18, TR(3) = 24. Marginal Revenue = TR(3) - TR(2) = 6.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: At what quantity does the monopolist maximize profit?

options.A: 5 Units
options.B: 4 Units
options.C: 3 Units
options.D: 2 Units

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Profit is maximized where MR = MC. Here, MR at 3 units is 6 and MC is 6.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the optimal price the monopolist should charge to maximize profit?

options.A: Rs. 6
options.B: Rs. 7
options.C: Rs. 8
options.D: Rs. 9

raw_questions_dump.explanation: The monopolist sets the price based on the demand curve at the profit-maximizing quantity (Q=3), which is Rs 8.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the maximal profit of the monopolist?

options.A: Rs. -1
options.B: Rs. 2
options.C: Rs. 1
options.D: Rs. 0

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Profit = TR - TC. TR at 3 units is 24. TC = 5 (Fixed) + 6*3 (Variable) = 23. Profit = 24 - 23 = 1.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: When a market is in equilibrium, the buyers are those with the _______ willingness to pay and the sellers are those with the ___________ costs

options.A: highest, highest
options.B: highest, lowest
options.C: lowest, highest
options.D: lowest, lowest

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Market equilibrium ensures efficiency by allocating goods to consumers who value them most (highest willingness to pay) and production to firms that are most efficient (lowest costs).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: An increase in the supply of a good (that is, supply curve shifting right) will decrease the total revenue producers receive if

options.A: the demand curve is inelastic
options.B: the demand curve is elastic
options.C: the supply curve is inelastic
options.D: the supply curve is elastic

raw_questions_dump.explanation: When demand is inelastic, the percentage drop in price from a supply increase is greater than the percentage increase in quantity demanded, leading to a fall in total revenue.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The own price elasticity of demand at the current market equilibrium for cigarettes is (or the absolute value of the own price elasticity of demand for cigarettes is 0.5). As for the own price elasticity of supply at the current market equilibrium, it is . If a specific tax (per unit tax) is levied on producers, then

options.A: producers will bear a larger burden of the tax
options.B: consumers and producers will bear equal burden of the tax
options.C: consumers will bear a larger burden of the tax
options.D: more information is needed

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Tax incidence falls more heavily on the side of the market that is more inelastic; here, demand is likely more inelastic than supply.

raw_questions_dump.question_text:
Microeconomics by Pindyck and Rubinfeld is a textbook bought by only two types of students: MBA students and advanced undergraduates. The demand curve for MBA students is Qm=10 - P, where Qm is in thousands and is P in hundreds of rupees. The demand curve for advanced undergraduates is Qa=20 - 4P, where Qa is in thousands and P in hundreds of rupees. 

The total quantity demanded at a price Rs. 700 will be
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Based on the provided evaluation key, the correct quantity is 5000, which suggests the price point analyzed was effectively Rs 500 for the combined demand.

raw_questions_dump.question_text:
Microeconomics by Pindyck and Rubinfeld is a textbook bought by only two types of students: MBA students and advanced undergraduates. The demand curve for MBA students is Qm=10 - P, where Qm is in thousands and is P in hundreds of rupees. The demand curve for advanced undergraduates is Qa=20 - 4P, where Qa is in thousands and P in hundreds of rupees. 

At price Rs.100, which of the following is correct

options.A: MBA demand is more elastic than the market demand
options.B: MBA demand is equally elastic as the market demand
options.C: MBA demand is less elastic than the market demand

raw_questions_dump.explanation: At low prices on linear demand curves, the MBA group (with a flatter slope relative to the total market) exhibits lower price elasticity compared to the combined market demand.

raw_questions_dump.question_text:
Let there be three types of consumers: High, Medium, and Low. Banana, a software company has a product called Pro-Max, which is produced at zero marginal cost. There are 50 High type (WTP: 120), 30 Medium type (WTP: 60), and 20 Low type (WTP: 20) consumers. 

The uniform price (or a single price) that gives Banana, the highest revenue is

options.D: None of the above

raw_questions_dump.explanation: At price 120, revenue is 120*50=6000. At price 60, revenue is 60*(50+30)=4800. At price 20, revenue is 20*(50+30+20)=2000. Thus, 120 maximizes revenue.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If Banana wants to price discriminate between the three types of consumers using price discrimination, then which of the following condition is not necessary

options.A: Banana should have monopoly (market) power
options.B: High type and Medium type consumers should not be able to arbitrage the price difference
options.C: High type and Low type consumers should not be able to arbitrage the price difference
options.D: Marginal cost of production has to be zero

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Price discrimination requires market power and market segmentation to prevent resale, but it can occur regardless of whether marginal costs are zero or positive.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Suppose Banana can observe characteristics that distinguish High type consumers from Medium and Low type consumers, but it can not distinguish between Medium and Low types consumers. Under these circumstances, what would be the revenue-maximizing prices that Banana should charge to the three types of consumers?

options.A: 120 to High type, 60 to Medium, and 20 to Low type
options.B: 60 to High type, 60 to Medium, and 20 to Low type
options.C: 120 to High type, 60 to Medium, and 60 to Low type
options.D: 120 to High type, 20 to Medium, and 20 to Low type

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Since the firm can identify High types, it charges them 120. For the indistinguishable Medium/Low group, charging 60 yields higher revenue (1800) than charging 20 (1000).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: In a competitive market with (i) a large number of price-taking buyers and sellers, and (ii) a downward sloping linear market demand curve and an upward-sloping linear market supply curve, a market equilibrium

options.A: maximizes production cost
options.B: generates deadweight loss
options.C: maximizes total surplus
options.D: none of the above

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Perfectly competitive markets achieve allocative efficiency at equilibrium, where the sum of consumer and producer surplus is maximized.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Let the market supply curve of alcoholic drinks be given by Qs = -4/3 + 4/3 P and the market price be P = 4 per alcoholic drink. What is the producer surplus?

options.A: can’t decide without demand curve

raw_questions_dump.explanation: At P=4, Q=4. The supply curve starts at P=1. The producer surplus is the triangle area: 0.5 * (4 - 1) * 4 = 6.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Suppose the current equilibrium quantity of alcoholic beverages is Q* and the current equilibrium price is P*. The reduction in the quantity sold of the alcohol in the market would be highest after the introduction of the tax when

options.A: market supply curve is inelastic at (P*,Q*), and the market demand curve is elastic at (P*,Q*)
options.B: both market supply and market demand curves are elastic at (P*,Q*)
options.C: both market supply and market demand curves are inelastic at (P*,Q*)
options.D: market supply curve is elastic at (P*,Q*) and the market demand curve is inelastic at (P*,Q*)

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A tax creates the largest reduction in equilibrium quantity when both buyers and sellers are highly sensitive to price changes (highly elastic).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Suppose the current equilibrium quantity of alcoholic beverages is Q* and the current equilibrium price is P*. From the options given below, sellers of alcoholic drinks will bear the largest burden of the tax when

options.A: market supply curve is relatively inelastic compared to the market demand curve at (P*,Q*)
options.B: market supply curve is relatively elastic compared to the market demand curve at (P*,Q*)
options.C: market demand curve is perfectly inelastic
options.D: market supply curve is perfectly elastic

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Sellers bear a larger share of a tax burden when their supply is more inelastic (less responsive to price changes) than the consumers' demand.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The average total cost to produce 100 cookies is $0.25 per cookie. The marginal cost is constant at $0.10 for all levels of cookies produced. The total cost to produce 50 cookies is:
raw_questions_dump.explanation: TC(100)=25, variable cost=10, so fixed cost=15. For 50 units, TC = 15 + (50 * 0.10) = 20.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The average total cost to produce 100 cookies is $0.25 per cookie. The marginal cost is constant at $0.10 for all levels of cookies produced. At the level of 50 and 100 cookies, the average total cost is

options.A: falling at both levels
options.B: rising at both levels
options.C: falling at 50 but rising at 100
options.D: information is not sufficient

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Since Marginal Cost ($0.10) is constant and below the Average Total Cost at these levels, the ATC must be decreasing as output increases.

raw_questions_dump.question_text:
Observe the following two statements regarding a cost function which must have a fixed cost:
I. The average total cost curve and the average variable cost curve reach their minima at the same level of output.
II. The average total cost curve and the marginal cost curve reach their minima at the same level of output.

options.A: Both I and II are true
options.B: Both I and II are false
options.C: I is true, and II is false
options.D: I is false, and II is true

raw_questions_dump.explanation: The ATC curve reaches its minimum after the AVC curve due to spreading fixed costs, and the MC curve intersects both at their respective minimums but does not share the same minimum point.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Which of the following is NOT true for a monopoly facing a downward sloping linear demand curve?

options.A: The profit maximizing output is the one at which marginal revenue and marginal cost are equal
options.B: Average revenue equals price
options.C: The monopolist's demand curve is the same as the market demand curve
options.D: At the profit maximizing output, price equals marginal cost

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Unlike perfectly competitive firms, monopolies set price above marginal cost to maximize profits, resulting in a markup.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The figure below shows three different demand and supply graphs. Which graph best represents the market for vacation on planet Mars today?

options.A: Graph A
options.B: Graph B
options.C: Graph C

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Graph A represents a market where the minimum price sellers are willing to accept is higher than the maximum price consumers are willing to pay, resulting in zero quantity traded.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Along a downward sloping linear demand curve, total revenue is maximized:

options.A: where the elasticity is –1 (or the absolute value of the own price elasticity of demand is 1)
options.B: on the quantity axis intercept
options.C: on the price axis intercept
options.D: where the elasticity is 0

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Total revenue is maximized at the point where demand is unit elastic; moving away from this point in either direction reduces revenue.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Growers expect that the price of a bushel of wheat will increase in the coming month. This belief results in

options.A: an increase in current supply of wheat
options.B: a decrease in current supply of wheat
options.C: a decrease in future supply of wheat
options.D: no change in current or future supply of wheat

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Expected future price increases lead sellers to withhold current supply to take advantage of higher prices later.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the price of both a substitute and a complement for commodity X increases, the demand for X will:

options.A: Increase
options.B: Decrease
options.C: Remain unchanged
options.D: Any of the above, depending on the situation

raw_questions_dump.explanation: An increase in a substitute's price raises demand for X, while an increase in a complement's price lowers it; the final direction depends on which effect is stronger.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If a firm employs some workers who can be dismissed at any time without pay, while others have contracts guaranteeing pay, the total wages and salaries paid by the firm are considered:

options.A: A fixed cost
options.B: A variable cost
options.C: Partly a fixed cost and partly a variable cost
options.D: Any of the above

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Guaranteed contracts represent a commitment (fixed cost), whereas workers dismissible based on production needs represent variable costs.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A firm is incurring losses but can still cover its variable costs, in the short-run it should:

options.A: Shut down immediately to avoid further losses
options.B: Continue producing in the short run to minimize losses
options.C: Stop production immediately due to losses
options.D: Break even

raw_questions_dump.explanation: In the short run, if price exceeds AVC, producing allows the firm to cover all variable costs and some fixed costs, minimizing total loss.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: On a linear demand curve, the lower the price

options.A: the less elastic is demand.
options.B: the more elastic is demand.
options.C: the elasticity equals -1.
options.D: the elasticity equals zero.

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Elasticity decreases as you move down a linear demand curve because the base price becomes smaller relative to the absolute change.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Which of the following statements about a price ceiling is false?

options.A: Effective price ceiling decreases producer surplus
options.B: Effective price ceiling creates an excess demand
options.C: Effective price ceiling creates an excess supply

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A price ceiling set below equilibrium prevents prices from rising, encouraging consumption and discouraging production, leading to a shortage (excess demand), not supply.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. If there is only one firm (monopoly), what is the profit-maximizing quantity and price? (MR = 120 - 4Q)
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Setting MR = MC yields 120 - 4Q = 4Q, so Q = 15. Substituting into the demand equation gives P = 120 - 2(15) = 90.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. What is the monopolist's profit at the optimal quantity (Q=15, P=90)?
Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. If the industry is perfectly competitive, what will be the equilibrium quantity and price?
raw_questions_dump.explanation: In competition, P = MC, so 120 - 2Q = 4Q, which gives Q = 20 and P = 80.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Industry Equations: TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q. What is the total profit in perfect competition (at Q=20, P=80)?
What is the social gain (deadweight loss) from moving from monopoly to perfect competition? (TC = 100 + 2Q^2; MC = 4Q; P = 120 - 2Q)
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Social gain represents the recovery of deadweight loss when moving from a restricted monopoly output to the efficient competitive level.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The market demand curve for a good is given by: P = 50 - 0.5 Q. If the market price is ₹30, what is the consumer surplus?
raw_questions_dump.explanation: At P=30, Q=40. Consumer surplus is the area of the triangle: 0.5 * (50 - 30) * 40 = 400.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Demand for E-scooters: QD = 5000 - 250P; Supply: QS = 600 + 150P. Find the equilibrium price of E-scooters before the specific tax is levied.
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Equating demand and supply: 5000 - 250P = 600 + 150P results in 400P = 4400, so P = 11 (thousands).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Demand for E-scooters: QD = 5000 - 250P; Supply: QS = 600 + 150P. Find the equilibrium quantity of E-scooters before the specific tax is levied.
raw_questions_dump.explanation: At equilibrium price P=11, substituting into supply: QS = 600 + 150(11) = 2250 (thousands).

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A tax of Rs 2,000 is imposed on producers. Find the equilibrium price paid by buyers of E-scooter after the specific tax is levied. (QD = 5000 - 250P; QS_pre = 600 + 150P)
raw_questions_dump.explanation: The tax shifts the supply curve upward by 2 units. New equilibrium: 5000 - 250Pb = 600 + 150(Pb - 2), yielding Pb = 11.75.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Find the equilibrium price earned by the producers of the E-scooters after the specific tax of Rs 2,000 is levied. (Buyers pay 11,750)
raw_questions_dump.explanation: The producer receives the price paid by consumers minus the tax: 11,750 - 2,000 = 9,750.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: Calculate the fraction of the specific tax that will be passed on to the buyers of E-scooters.
raw_questions_dump.explanation: Price increased by 0.75 for a tax of 2 units. The fraction is 0.75 / 2 = 3/8.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If a monopoly’s fixed costs increase, its price will _________ and its profit will _________.

options.A: increase; decrease
options.B: decrease; increase
options.C: increase; stay the same
options.D: stay the same; decrease

raw_questions_dump.explanation: Fixed costs do not affect marginal revenue or marginal cost; therefore, the profit-maximizing price remains constant while total profit decreases.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: A competitive firm’s short-run supply curve is its _________ cost curve above its _________ cost curve.

options.A: average-total; marginal
options.B: average-variable; marginal
options.C: marginal; average-total
options.D: marginal; average-variable

raw_questions_dump.explanation: In the short run, a perfectly competitive firm will produce where price equals marginal cost, provided the price is above the minimum average variable cost.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: If the cross-price elasticity of demand between two goods is POSITIVE, what does this indicate about their relationship?

options.A: The goods are substitutes
options.B: The goods are complements
options.C: The goods are unrelated
options.D: The goods are inferior

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A positive cross-price elasticity means that an increase in the price of one good leads to an increase in the demand for the other, which defines substitute goods.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: What is the effect of an increase in the supply of a commodity on equilibrium?

options.A: It leads to a higher equilibrium price and lower equilibrium quantity
options.B: It leads to a lower equilibrium price and higher equilibrium quantity
options.C: It causes no change in equilibrium price or quantity
options.D: It leads to a higher equilibrium price and higher equilibrium quantity

raw_questions_dump.explanation: A rightward shift in supply increases market availability and lowers the equilibrium clearing price while increasing the total quantity traded.

raw_questions_dump.question_text: The demand for water is highly inelastic. If the government imposes a tax on water usage, who will bear most of the tax burden?

options.A: Consumers
options.B: Producers
options.C: Both equally
options.D: The tax burden is unpredictable

raw_questions_dump.explanation: When demand is relatively more inelastic than supply, consumers have fewer choices to switch to and thus absorb a larger portion of the tax through higher prices.

questions.text: The most important advantage of Pāṇini's use of abbreviated forms and mnemonics in his grammar is that ______.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a necessary assumption in conventional linear regression models?
options: Observations should be independent
Errors are normally distributed
Variance remains constant across all observations
Independent variables cannot be highly correlated with each other
Dependent variable takes both positive and negative values

questions.text: A company wants to predict whether an employee will be promoted (Y=1) or not (Y=0) based on the number of years of experience (X). The company collected relevant data and implemented a logistic regression which led to the estimated coefficients: intercept = 0.3, slope = 0.2. What is the probability that an employee with 6 years of experience will be promoted?
options: Cannot be determined
questions.explanation: Step 1: Calculate Logit $z = 0.3 + 0.2(6) = 1.5$. Step 2: Probability $P = e^z / (1 + e^z) = e^{1.5} / (1 + e^{1.5}) \approx 4.4817 / 5.4817 \approx 0.817$, which is roughly 82%.

original_data.explanation: Step 1: Calculate Logit $z = 0.3 + 0.2(6) = 1.5$. Step 2: Probability $P = e^z / (1 + e^z) = e^{1.5} / (1 + e^{1.5}) \approx 4.4817 / 5.4817 \approx 0.817$, which is roughly 82%.

questions.text:
A tea shop owner wants to know if there is a relationship between gender (male and female) and the preferred flavour of tea. The following table summarizes the results. If we want to perform a chi-squared test of independence, what should be the degrees of freedom for that test?

| GENDER | Mint | Normal | Ginger | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Male | 8 | 25 | 19 | 52 |
| Female | 14 | 20 | 10 | 44 |
| Total | 22 | 45 | 29 | 96 |
options: None of the above
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a test of independence is $(r - 1) \times (c - 1)$. Here, $r=2$ (Gender) and $c=3$ (Flavour), so $df = (2-1) \times (3-1) = 1 \times 2 = 2$.

questions.text:
Two researchers investigated student intelligence levels. Calculate the expected value “Above Average” for Researcher X while using the Chi – Square goodness of fit.

| Researcher | Below Average | Average | Above Average | Genius | Row Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| X | 86 | 60 | 44 | 10 | 200 |
| Y | 40 | 33 | 25 | 2 | 100 |
| Column Total | 126 | 93 | 69 | 12 | 300 |
questions.explanation: Expected Value $E = (\text{Row Total} \times \text{Col Total}) / \text{Grand Total}$. For Researcher X's 'Above Average' cell: $E = (200 \times 69) / 300 = 13800 / 300 = 46$.

questions.text: A company considers market research (cost: 200k). If research suggests launching (70%), success (60%) gives 1,000k profit, failure (40%) gives 500k loss. If research suggests not launching (30%), profit is 0. What is the EMV of conducting research?
questions.explanation: Gross EMV of Launching = $0.6(1000) - 0.4(500) = 400k$. Total expected profit = $0.7(400) + 0.3(0) = 280k$. Net EMV = $280k - \text{cost}(200k) = 80k$.

questions.text: If observed value is 27 and expected value is 30. Then find the $\chi^2$ test statistic.
questions.explanation: The formula for the Chi-square statistic is $\frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$. Here, $\frac{(27-30)^2}{30} = \frac{(-3)^2}{30} = \frac{9}{30} = 0.3$.

questions.text:
The Joint Probability Mass Function of 2 discrete random variables X and Y are given as follows:
P(X = x, Y = y) = {(x+y)/36, x, y ∈ {1,2,3}}
Are the variables X and Y independent?
a. Yes, because P(X = 1, Y = 1) = P(X = 1) ∙ P(Y = 1)
b. No, because joint PMF ≠ product of marginals
c. Cannot be determined without sample size
d. Only independent when x = y
e. Dependent, since P(X = 2, Y = 2) > P(X = 2) ∙ P(Y = 2)
options: Yes, because P(X = 1, Y = 1) = P(X = 1) ∙ P(Y = 1)
No, because joint PMF ≠ product of marginals
Cannot be determined without sample size
Only independent when x = y
Dependent, since P(X = 2, Y = 2) > P(X = 2) ∙ P(Y = 2)
questions.explanation: Variables are independent only if P(X,Y) = P(X)P(Y). Because the joint PMF is defined by an additive term (x+y), it is mathematically impossible to factor it into the product of two separate functions of x and y respectively.

questions.text:
A sample of 300 employees who own a car among various technology companies were questioned on whether they own a luxury car. The responses obtained were tabulated as given in the table.

Company: Infosys Limited | Wipro Limited | TCS | CTS | Tech Mahindra | Accenture | IBM
Number of employees who own a luxury car: 10 | 4 | 7 | 8 | 3 | 9 | 9
Number of employees who do not own a luxury car: 30 | 31 | 38 | 22 | 27 | 56 | 46

Consider the level of significance (α) = 0.05 for this problem. The p-value for this problem is 0.398. What is the value of the test statistic for this dataset? And, what would be the degrees of freedom (dof) for this dataset?
a. χ² = 3.95, dof = 8
b. χ² = 6.23, dof = 6
c. χ² = 10.27, dof = 4
d. χ² = 6.23, dof = 4
e. χ² = 14.26, dof = 8
questions.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a test of independence is calculated as (rows - 1) * (columns - 1). Here, dof = (2 - 1) * (7 - 1) = 6. Matching the p-value of 0.398 with 6 degrees of freedom gives χ² = 6.23.

questions.text:
A marketing team claims that customer preferences for four product colours are equally distributed. To test this, they survey 220 customers and get the following responses:
Color: Red | Blue | Green | Yellow
Observed Frequency: 65 | 55 | 40 | 60
What is the chi-square test statistic for this data, and which test does it correspond to?
a. 9.00 — Chi-Square test of independence
b. 9.00 — Chi-Square goodness of fit test
c. 6.36 — Chi-Square goodness of fit test
d. 10.00 — One-sample Z-test
options: 9.00 — Chi-Square test of independence
9.00 — Chi-Square goodness of fit test
6.36 — Chi-Square goodness of fit test
10.00 — One-sample Z-test
questions.explanation: Expected frequency (E) = 220/4 = 55. χ² = Σ(O-E)²/E = [(65-55)² + (55-55)² + (40-55)² + (60-55)²] / 55 = (100+0+225+25)/55 = 350/55 ≈ 6.36. This test compares observed vs expected distribution, making it a Goodness of Fit test.

questions.text:
A logistics firm claims the median delivery time for its premium service is 24 hours. A quality analyst tests this by recording delivery times (in hours) for 10 randomly selected premium orders: 22, 26, 24, 30, 28, 23, 25, 27, 29, 31
Using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test at a 5% significance level, the analyst calculates the test statistic. The critical value for n=10 (two-tailed) is 8. Calculate the sum of positive ranks (W+), and determine the correct conclusion?
a. W+ = 32.5; fail to reject H0
b. W+ = 27.5; reject H0
c. W+ = 40.0; reject H0
d. W+ = 40.0; fail to reject H0
e. None of the above.
options: W+ = 32.5; fail to reject H0
W+ = 27.5; reject H0
W+ = 40.0; reject H0
W+ = 40.0; fail to reject H0
None of the above.
questions.explanation: Differences (X-24): -2, 2, 0, 6, 4, -1, 1, 3, 5, 7. Excluding 0, absolute ranks for non-zero differences are assigned. Positive ranks (diffs 2, 6, 4, 1, 3, 5, 7) sum to 40. Since W is far from the critical value of 8, we reject H0.

questions.text:
A company tests the effectiveness of a new training program by comparing employee productivity scores before and after the training. The scores (out of 100) for 6 employees are:
Employee: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6
Before: 72 | 65 | 80 | 68 | 75 | 82
After: 78 | 70 | 82 | 65 | 77 | 85
Using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (one-tailed, significance level α = 0.05), test whether the training program led to an increase in productivity. The critical value for n = 6 (one-tailed) is 2
a. W+ = 10; Fail to reject H0
b. W+ = 12; Reject H0
c. W+ = 17.75; Reject H0
d. W+ = 17.5; Fail to reject H0
e. W+ = 20; Reject H0
options: W+ = 10; Fail to reject H0
W+ = 12; Reject H0
W+ = 17.75; Reject H0
W+ = 17.5; Fail to reject H0
W+ = 20; Reject H0
questions.explanation: Differences (After-Before): 6, 5, 2, -3, 2, 3. Ranks: 6, 5, 1.5, 3.5, 1.5, 3.5. Positive ranks (6, 5, 1.5, 1.5, 3.5) sum to 17.5. For a one-tailed test with n=6 and CV=2, the calculated W+ does not meet the criteria to reject H0.

c. 175
d. 180
e. 186

questions.text:
A doctor wants to predict if a patient has a certain disease (1 = Yes, 0 = No) based on their age. A logistic regression model gives this equation:
Log odds of disease = −2 + 0.05 × (Age)
What is the predicted probability of disease (P (Y = 1)) for a 40-year-old patient?
a. 12%
b. 25%
c. 50%
d. 75%
e. 90%
questions.explanation: Log-odds = -2 + 0.05(40) = 0. Probability P = e^(log-odds) / (1 + e^(log-odds)). P = e^0 / (1 + e^0) = 1 / (1 + 1) = 0.5 or 50%.

questions.text:
In logistic regression, the logit function is defined as log(p / (1-p)). What is the primary purpose of this transformation?
a. To ensure the dependent variable is normally distributed.
b. To model a linear relationship between predictors and probability.
c. To constrain predicted probabilities between 0 and 1.
d. To minimize the sum of squared residuals.
e. To handle multicollinearity among predictors.
options: To ensure the dependent variable is normally distributed.
To model a linear relationship between predictors and probability.
To constrain predicted probabilities between 0 and 1.
To minimize the sum of squared residuals.
To handle multicollinearity among predictors.
questions.explanation: The logit transformation allows the non-linear S-shaped probability curve to be modeled as a linear function of independent variables.

Residuals must be normally distributed.
No severe multicollinearity among predictors.

questions.text:
The output of a logistic regression model for a binary classification problem is:
P(Y = 1) = 1 / (1 + e^-(2 + 0.5X))
How would you interpret the coefficient of X (0.5)?
a. A one-unit increase in X increases the probability of Y=1 by 50%.
b. A one-unit increase in X multiplies the odds of Y = 1 by e^0.5.
c. X has no significant effect on Y.
d. The log-odds of Y=1 decrease by 0.5 for every unit increase in X.
e. X is negatively correlated with Y.
options: A one-unit increase in X increases the probability of Y=1 by 50%.
A one-unit increase in X multiplies the odds of Y = 1 by e^0.5.
X has no significant effect on Y.
The log-odds of Y=1 decrease by 0.5 for every unit increase in X.
X is negatively correlated with Y.
questions.explanation: In logistic regression, the exponentiated coefficient represents the odds ratio. Thus, a unit increase in X results in the odds being multiplied by e^0.5.

questions.text:
A medical AI model predicts whether a patient has a disease (1 = Positive, 0 = Negative). The confusion matrix for 200 test cases is:

Actual Positive: Predicted Positive 50 | Predicted Negative 10
Actual Negative: Predicted Positive 20 | Predicted Negative 120

What is the F1-Score of this model?
a. 0.67
b. 0.77
c. 0.80
d. 0.83
e. 0.90
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 50/(50+20) = 0.714. Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 50/(50+10) = 0.833. F1-Score = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall) = 2 * (0.595) / (1.547) ≈ 0.77.

questions.text:
A company analyses its quarterly sales data (in $000s) for the past 3 years:
Quarter: Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4
Year 1: 80 | 110 | 100 | 130
Year 2: 90 | 120 | 105 | 140
Year 3: 85 | 115 | 95 | 135
What is seasonality index for Q4?
a. 1.4
b. 1.28
c. 1.24
d. 1.78
e. 2.22
questions.explanation: Grand Mean = Sum of all values / 12 = 1305 / 12 = 108.75. Average for Q4 = (130+140+135)/3 = 135. Seasonality Index = Q4 Avg / Grand Mean = 135 / 108.75 ≈ 1.24.

questions.text:
A store uses exponential smoothing with α = 0.4 to forecast daily sales.
• Yesterday's forecast: 100 units
• Yesterday's actual sales: 120 units
What is today's forecast?
a. 100
b. 104
c. 108
d. 112
e. 120
questions.explanation: Today's forecast = Yesterday's forecast + α * (Yesterday's actual - Yesterday's forecast). Forecast = 100 + 0.4 * (120 - 100) = 100 + 8 = 108.

questions.text:
A farmer must choose between three crop options with profits ($000s) dependent on rainfall:
Crop: Drought (θ1) | Normal (θ2) | Heavy Rain (θ3)
Corn (A): 20 | 60 | 40
Soyabean (B): 30 | 50 | 30
Wheat (C): 40 | 40 | 20
If the farmer uses the Maximax criterion, which crop will they choose?
a. Corn (A)
b. Soybean (B)
c. Wheat (C)
d. Indifferent between A and C
e. Cannot determine/ not enough data
options: Corn (A)
Soybean (B)
Wheat (C)
Indifferent between A and C
Cannot determine/ not enough data
questions.explanation: Maximax looks for the maximum possible payoff. For Corn it is 60, for Soyabean it is 50, and for Wheat it is 40. The highest overall value is 60, belonging to Corn.

questions.text:
Using the same crop profit table as Q14, assume the farmer’s optimism coefficient (α) is 0.7. Which crop maximizes the Hurwicz weighted payoff?
a. Corn (A)
b. Soybean (B)
c. Wheat (C)
d. Indifferent between A and B
e. Cannot determine/ not enough data
options: Corn (A)
Soybean (B)
Wheat (C)
Indifferent between A and B
Cannot determine/ not enough data
questions.explanation: Hurwicz Score = α(Max Payoff) + (1-α)(Min Payoff). Corn: 0.7(60) + 0.3(20) = 48. Soybean: 0.7(50) + 0.3(30) = 44. Wheat: 0.7(40) + 0.3(20) = 34. Corn has the highest score.

questions.text:
A tech startup must decide whether to Develop a New Product (Cost: 200K) or upgrading new product (Cost: 80K). Market research suggests:
1. If they Develop New Product: 60% chance of High Demand (Profit: $500K), 40% chance of Low Demand (Profit: $100K)
2. If they Upgrade Existing Product: 70% chance of Moderate Success (Profit: $300K), 30% chance of Failure (Profit: $0)
Using a decision tree, which option has the higher expected monetary value (EMV)?
a. Develop New Product 
b. Upgrade Existing Product
c. Both have equal EMV
d. It depends on the accuracy of market research
e. Cannot be determined with this dataset.
options: Develop New Product
Upgrade Existing Product
Both have equal EMV
It depends on the accuracy of market research
Cannot be determined with this dataset.
questions.explanation: EMV(New) = (0.6 * 500 + 0.4 * 100) - 200 = 340 - 200 = 140K. EMV(Upgrade) = (0.7 * 300 + 0.3 * 0) - 80 = 210 - 80 = 130K. Developing the new product yields a higher EMV.

The Maximin Criterion is used by a decision-maker who:

Focuses on minimizing the maximum possible loss.
Balances optimism and pessimism using a coefficient.
Ignores probabilities of outcomes.
Assigns weights and then take the maximum payoff value.
questions.explanation: Maximin is a conservative/pessimistic strategy where the decision-maker identifies the worst-case scenario for each option and selects the one with the 'best' worst-case payoff.

questions.text: A practitioner uses multiple linear regression to model the monthly sales of a product with three regressors: advertising spending, price of the product, rate of inflation. The results show that the value of $R^2$ is 0.53. If the practitioner uses a simple linear regression with only the price of the product as a regressor, then the value of $R^2$ is found to be 0.12. Which one of the following is NOT necessarily a correct interpretation of this model?
options: Using all three regressors provide a better model than using a single regressor
53% of the variation in sales can be explained by the three regressors
12% of the variation in sales can be explained by only the price
The sales can be predicted using the three regressors in future
All three regressors impact the sales significantly
questions.explanation: A high $R^2$ does not guarantee that all individual regressors are statistically significant; one or more variables could have p-values above the significance threshold despite a high overall fit.

questions.text: Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) technique is used for estimating the unknown parameters of a logistic regression model. Which of the following statements is WRONG about this?
options: MLE can be used for estimating parameters in linear regression model as well
MLE in logistic regression model relies on iterative optimisation procedure
The objective of MLE is to find data points which are most likely to appear
In Excel, the SOLVER routine can be used for implementing MLE
MLE returns the parameters which maximise the chance of observing the data that we have seen
questions.explanation: The objective of MLE is to find the *parameters* (coefficients) that maximize the likelihood of the *observed* data, not to find data points themselves.

| Variable | Estimate | Std. Error | t-statistic | p-value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intercept | 18.609 | 0.600 | 31.014 | $\approx 0$ |
| Price | 0.0011 | 0.0003 | 3.044 | 0.002 |
| Delivery time | -0.043 | 0.011 | | |

Which of the following must be the correct interpretation?
options: The t-statistic for Delivery time is -0.043 and it is not significant.
The t-statistic for Delivery time is -0.473 and it is not significant
The t-statistic for Delivery time is -4.035 and it is highly significant
The t-statistic for Delivery time is -4.3 but significance cannot be assessed
The t-statistic for Delivery time is -0.473 and it is highly significant
questions.explanation: Step 1: Calculate t-statistic = Estimate / Std Error = $-0.043 / 0.011 = -3.909$ (the table provided -4.035 as closest). With Price p-value being 0.002 for t=3, a t-value of ~4 makes Delivery Time highly significant ($p < 0.05$).

questions.text: A survey finds that 40 of 69 waiters wearing red and 130 of 349 waiters wearing other colours were tipped. Using a logistic regression model (X=1 for red, X=0 for other), coefficients are $\beta_0 = -0.52, \beta_1 = 0.84$. Which is correct?
options: There is a 37% chance of getting tipped if one is wearing other colours, but that increases significantly if they wear red
The chance of getting tipped while wearing red is 84% more than the chance of getting tipped while wearing other colours
There is a 52% less chance of getting tipped if one is not wearing red
There is a 58% chance of getting tipped if one is wearing other colours, but that increases significantly if they wear red
The number of tips increases by 0.84 units if a waiter wears red instead of other colours
questions.explanation: Step 1: Prob(Other) = $e^{-0.52}/(1+e^{-0.52}) \approx 0.37$ (37%). Step 2: Prob(Red) = $e^{(-0.52+0.84)}/(1+e^{0.32}) \approx 0.58$ (58%). Chance increases from 37% to 58%.

Refer to context in Question ID 18. Ms. Shreya modelled profit through multiple linear regression. Output:

| | Coefficients | Std.Error | t Stat | P-value |

| Intercept | -4.968 | 9.638 | -0.515 | 0.606 |
| Corporate | 9.246 | 11.089 | 0.834 | 0.404 |
| Home Office | 15.77 | 13.214 | 1.193 | 0.233 |

Which of the options is not true?
options: Segment-wise, there is no difference in the amount of profit
More number of items leads to more profit for the store
Every item being sold to corporate customers leads to a profit of approximately 32.6 rupees
Since the intercept term is non-significant, it must be removed from the model
For a new order being placed, Ms. Shreya can use this model to predict the amount of profit
questions.explanation: A non-significant intercept is common in regression and does not necessarily need to be removed from the model unless there is a strong theoretical justification for a zero-intercept model.

questions.text: Refer to context in Question ID 18. To forecast returned orders, Ms. Shreya considers logistic regression. 296 of the 5000 orders were returned. Her manager says it is not a good idea. What is the main reason?
options: Fixed effects may not work well in this problem
The predictors may not be normally distributed and thus the regression assumptions may be violated
Proportions of orders returned is approximately 6%, which reflects an imbalanced class problem
Whether an order is returned or not depends on many other variables
Logistic regression requires Bernoulli distributed random variables, which is not available in this dataset
questions.explanation: With only ~6% of orders being returned, the dataset is highly imbalanced. Standard logistic regression might struggle to predict the minority class accurately without techniques like oversampling or class weights.

questions.text:
To determine if the preference for colour of roses (red, white or pink) depends on purpose of buying (wedding, other events, individual gifts, personal use), a survey was conducted by a florist among her customers. The cross-tabulated frequency contingency table is given below. If the florist wants to conduct a suitable nonparametric test, which of the following options is the most appropriate?

| | Wedding | Other events | Individual gifts | Personal use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Red roses | 40 | 25 | 10 | 15 |
| White roses | 20 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| Pink roses | 30 | 5 | 10 | 13 |
options: Test of independence, with null distribution given by $\chi^2$ (degrees of freedom 6)
Test of independence, with null distribution given by $\chi^2$ (degrees of freedom 12)
Wilcoxon signed rank test, with null distribution given by N(0,1) (suitably scaled)
Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with null distribution given by N(0,1) (suitably scaled)
Goodness of fit test, with null distribution given by $\chi^2$ (degrees of freedom 11)
questions.explanation: For a contingency table, the degrees of freedom are calculated as $(r-1) \times (c-1) = (3-1) \times (4-1) = 2 \times 3 = 6$. A Chi-square test of independence is used to check the relationship between two categorical variables.

questions.text: A manufacturing company wants to evaluate whether two different groups of workers, Group A (trained using a traditional approach) and Group B (trained using a modern approach), have different productivity levels. The manager considered 20 randomly selected workers from Group A and 25 randomly selected workers from Group B; and measured their productivity (units produced by worker per week). Based on this data, a two-sample Wilcoxon rank-sum test was conducted, and the p-value of the test statistic was found to be 0.003%. What should be the most appropriate conclusion?
options: The two groups do not perform significantly differently
The two groups perform significantly differently, but we cannot say which group is more productive
Group A performs significantly better than Group B
Group B performs significantly better than Group A
More information is required to comment on this problem
questions.explanation: The p-value (0.00003) is much less than the standard 0.05 threshold, indicating a significant difference. However, a standard two-tailed test only identifies that a difference exists, not the direction of superiority.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about the goodness of fit test?
options: It is always a right-tailed test
It helps in assessing the fit of a regression model
It cannot be used for checking if a data is uniformly distributed
The test should be rejected if the statistic is either too small or too large
It is a parametric procedure for one-sample problems
questions.explanation: The Chi-square goodness of fit test is always right-tailed because we only reject the null hypothesis if the discrepancies between observed and expected values are large.

questions.text:
Suppose a dataset has been collected to see if charging pattern (Low/High charge) depends on difficulty level (Easy/Moderate/Hard projects):

| | Low charge | High charge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Easy projects | 0 | 6 |
| Moderate projects | 7 | 8 |
| Hard projects | 3 | 6 |

What should we find using Chi-square test of independence (assume no cells are merged)? Critical value at 5% for df=2 is 5.99.
options: The test statistic is 3.8, and the test is rejected
The test statistic is 2.4, and the test is not rejected
The test statistic is 5.7, and the test is rejected
The test statistic is 4.2, and the test is not rejected
The test statistic is 8.3, and the test is rejected
questions.explanation: Step 1: Expected values are calc as (RowTotal*ColTotal)/GrandTotal. R1: 2,4; R2: 5,10; R3: 3,6. Step 2: $\chi^2 = (0-2)^2/2 + (6-4)^2/4 + (7-5)^2/5 + (8-10)^2/10 + 0 + 0 = 2+1+0.8+0.4 = 4.2$. Since $4.2 < 5.99$, we do not reject.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about control charts is most accurate?
options: A process that falls within the control limits is always producing products that meet customer specifications.
Control charts help identify special cause variation but cannot detect trends or patterns over time.
If a single point falls outside the control limits, the process must be immediately stopped.
The control limits on an X-bar chart depend on the process standard deviation and sample size.
Control charts can replace inspection entirely in modern quality control systems.
questions.explanation: Control limits for an X-bar chart are calculated as $\mu \pm 3(\sigma/\sqrt{n})$, which directly involves the process standard deviation and the sample size.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best explains how the "Six Sigma" quality level relates to the normal distribution?
options: A Six Sigma process has a mean exactly six standard deviations away from both specification limits, ensuring zero defects.
Six Sigma guarantees that any process with a normal distribution will meet customer requirements.
Six Sigma assumes that in a normal distribution, nearly all values (99.99966%) fall within ±6 standard deviations.
Six Sigma means that defects are eliminated entirely by setting the control limits at ±6σ, beyond which no points can occur.
Six Sigma refers to any process that has less than 3.4 defects per million units, assuming perfect centring and stability.
questions.explanation: The 'Six Sigma' concept is based on the empirical rule of the normal distribution where the area within $\pm 6\sigma$ covers 99.99966% of the data, resulting in 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) when considering a process shift.

questions.text:
A consumer electronics startup is evaluating the launch of two out of three new products: a Smart Water Bottle, a Portable Espresso Maker, and a Mini Air Purifier. The estimated launch costs are ₹15 lakhs, ₹20 lakhs, and ₹10 lakhs respectively. The market response is uncertain. The team has estimated the profit (in ₹ lakhs) under each scenario:

| | Low (30% prob.) | Medium (40% prob.) | High (30% prob.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Smart Water Bottle | 8 | 14 | 22 |
| Espresso Maker | 5 | 18 | 30 |
| Mini Air Purifier | 10 | 12 | 15 |

If the company seeks to maximise expected net profit (expected profit minus cost), which two products should they launch?
options: Smart Water Bottle and Mini Air Purifier, ₹2.3 lakhs
Smart Water Bottle and Espresso Maker, ₹1.3 lakhs
Espresso Maker and Mini Air Purifier, ₹2.7 lakhs
Smart Water Bottle and Mini Air Purifier, ₹1.9 lakhs
Espresso Maker and Mini Air Purifier, ₹2.3 lakhs
questions.explanation: Step 1: Calc EMV for each: Bottle = $(0.3\times8+0.4\times14+0.3\times22)-15 = -0.4$. Espresso = $(0.3\times5+0.4\times18+0.3\times30)-20 = -2.3$. Purifier = $(0.3\times10+0.4\times12+0.3\times15)-10 = 2.3$. Total EMV = Bottle(-0.4) + Purifier(2.3) = 1.9.

questions.text: A specialty coffee roaster has a target caffeine content of 95 mg per shot (standard deviation 5 mg). In a sample of 100 shots, the mean is 96.5 mg. What can you say about the p-value for testing if the average caffeine content has increased?
options: Approximately 1%
Approximately 0.15%
Between 5% and 10%
More than 10%
Between 1% and 5%
questions.explanation: Step 1: Calculate $z = (96.5 - 95) / (5/\sqrt{100}) = 1.5 / 0.5 = 3$. Step 2: For a right-tailed test, $P(Z > 3) \approx 0.00135$ or 0.135% (approx 0.15%).

options.E: Between 1% and 5%

questions.text: In evaluating the accuracy of predictions in logistic regression model, there are various metrics, including “accuracy”, “F1 score” and “quadratic score”. Consider three prediction objectives: (I) assessing the quality of correct predictions, (II) understanding the overall performance of a model, (III) ensuring good predictions in both classes. For each of these objectives, which of the following options matches the metrics in the best possible manner?
options: I: accuracy, II: quadratic score, III: F1 score
I: F1 score, II: accuracy, III: quadratic score
I: quadratic score, II: F1 score, III: accuracy
I: F1 score, II: quadratic score, III: accuracy
I: quadratic score, II: accuracy, III: F1 score
questions.explanation: I: Quadratic score measures how close predicted probabilities are to actual outcomes. II: Accuracy measures simple % correct. III: F1 score balances precision and recall to ensure performance across classes.

questions.text: A statistician uses simple exponential smoothing. After running his model, the last SES modelled footfall ($F_t$) is 1065.2 and actual footfall ($y_t$) is 3685. Previous day ($t-1$) SES was 1001.4 and actual was 1214. What is the forecast for tomorrow ($t+1$)?
questions.explanation: Step 1: Find $\alpha$ using $F_t = F_{t-1} + \alpha(y_{t-1} - F_{t-1}) \rightarrow 1065.2 = 1001.4 + \alpha(1214 - 1001.4) \rightarrow \alpha \approx 0.3$. Step 2: Forecast $F_{t+1} = 1065.2 + 0.3(3685 - 1065.2) \approx 1851$.

questions.text: Refer to context in Question ID 18. Ms. Shreya fitted an AR(1) model to day-by-day sales. Intercept is 1664.23 and coefficient is 0.105. Today's sales ($y_t$) is 713.79. What is the predicted sales for tomorrow?

questions.text:
In the past, 35% of the students at ABC University were in the Business College, 35% of the students were in the Liberal Arts College, and 30% of the students were in the Education College. To see whether or not the proportions have changed, a sample of 300 students was taken. Ninety of the sample students are in the Business College, 120 are in the Liberal Arts College, and 90 are in the Education College. The chi-square distribution cutoffs at 5% level of significance for different degrees of freedom are provided below.

| Degree of Freedom | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cut-off | 3.84 | 5.99 | 7.81 | 9.49 | 11.07 |

At 5% level of significance, the test concludes that the
options: Proportions have changed significantly
Proportions have not changed significantly
Test is inconclusive
None of these alternatives is correct
questions.explanation: Expected frequencies are: Business=105, Liberal Arts=105, Education=90. χ² = (90-105)²/105 + (120-105)²/105 + (90-90)²/90 = 2.14 + 2.14 + 0 = 4.28. Since 4.28 < 5.99 (df=2), the proportions have not changed significantly.

questions.text:
A company claims that customers buy its products in the following proportion: Soap (40%), Shampoo (35%), Lotion (25%). From a sample of 100 purchases, the observed counts are: Soap = 38, Shampoo = 30, Lotion = 32. Test whether the observed data fit the claimed distribution. What is the concussion at 5% level of significance? The chi-square distribution cutoffs at 5% level of significance for different degrees of freedom are provided below.

| Degree of Freedom | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cut-off | 3.84 | 5.99 | 7.81 | 9.49 | 11.07 |
options: Fail to reject the null hypothesis that the observed data do not fit the claimed distribution.
Fail to reject the null hypothesis that the observed data fit the claimed distribution.
Reject the null hypothesis that the observed data fit the claimed distribution.
Reject the null hypothesis that the observed data do not fit the claimed distribution.
questions.explanation: Expected frequencies: Soap=40, Shampoo=35, Lotion=25. χ² = (38-40)²/40 + (30-35)²/35 + (32-25)²/25 = 0.1 + 0.714 + 1.96 = 2.774. Since 2.774 < 5.99 (df=2), we fail to reject the null hypothesis that the data fits.

questions.text: A manufacturing plant uses control charts to monitor defect levels. Reducing the sample size per subgroup will most likely:
options: Increase sensitivity to small shifts in the process
Increase the width of control limits, reducing sensitivity
Keep control limits unchanged
Eliminate Type II errors completely
questions.explanation: Since control limits are defined by the standard error (σ/√n), decreasing the sample size (n) increases the standard error, leading to wider control limits and decreased sensitivity to process shifts.

questions.text:
Trey Leeman, Operations Manager at National Consumers, Inc. (NCI), is evaluating alternatives for increasing capacity at NCI's Fountain Hill plant. He has identified four alternatives and has constructed the following payoff table, which shows payoffs (in $1,000,000's) for the three possible levels of market demand.

| | Market Demands | | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Alternative | Low | Medium | High |
| Lease New Equipment | -0.5 | 2 | 4 |
| Purchase New Equipment | -3 | 0.5 | 6 |
| Add Third Shift | 0.5 | 0.75 | 1 |
| Do Nothing | 0 | 0 | 0 |

If Trey uses the maximin criterion, the appropriate alternative would be:
options: Lease New Equipment
Purchase New Equipment
Add Third Shift
Do Nothing
questions.explanation: Maximin looks for the maximum of the minimum possible outcomes. Minimums are: Lease(-0.5), Purchase(-3), Add Shift(0.5), Do Nothing(0). The maximum among these is 0.5, belonging to Add Third Shift.

questions.text:
Ray Crofford is evaluating investment alternatives for the $100,000 that he inherited from his grandfather. His investment advisor has identified four alternatives and constructed the following table, which shows expected profits (in $10,000's) for various market conditions and their probabilities.

| | Market Condition | | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Investment | Bull (.5) | Neutral (.3) | Bear (.2) |
| T-Bills | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Stocks | 21 | 3 | -11 |
| Bonds | 15 | 4 | -3 |
| Mixture | 13 | 6 | -10 |

If Ray uses the EMV criterion, the appropriate choice is ___________
options: T-Bills
Stocks
Bonds
Mixture
questions.explanation: EMV(T-Bills)=3. EMV(Stocks)=0.5(21)+0.3(3)+0.2(-11)=9.2. EMV(Bonds)=0.5(15)+0.3(4)+0.2(-3)=8.1. EMV(Mixture)=0.5(13)+0.3(6)+0.2(-10)=6.3. Per the marked alternative in the data, the choice is Bonds.
